content_en:
The biographies of the Successors (tabi'un) — the generation of Muslims who learned directly from the Companions but did not know the Prophet personally — occupy a substantial portion of Tabaqat al-Kabir. This generation was crucial to the transmission of Islamic knowledge, as they were the primary recipients and transmitters of the Companions' knowledge and the teachers of the great hadith collectors of the third century AH.

The Definition of the Tabi'un

Ibn Sa'd is precise about the definition of the Successors. A person is counted among the tabi'un if they met at least one Companion as a Muslim (i.e., in a Muslim capacity, not merely as a historical encounter). The bar for being counted as a Companion is similarly precise: one must have met the Prophet as a Muslim and died a Muslim. These definitional questions had practical consequences for hadith evaluation, since Companion status carried a presumption of complete reliability that Successor status did not.

The tabi'un were not a homogeneous group. They included the great scholars of Madinah (Sa'id ibn al-Musayyab, Urwa ibn al-Zubayr, Nafi the freed slave of Ibn Umar, and others) who became the sources of the Madinan legal tradition; the scholars of Makkah (Mujahid, Ata ibn Abi Rabah, Tawus, Ikrimah) who formed the Makkan legal school; the scholars of Kufa (Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, Alqama ibn Qays, al-Sha'bi) who became the sources of the Iraqi/Hanafi tradition; and the scholars of Basra (Hasan al-Basri, Muhammad ibn Sirin, Ibn Sirin's contemporaries) who formed one of the richest centers of early Islamic scholarship.

Sa'id ibn al-Musayyab

Among the biographies of Successors in Tabaqat al-Kabir, that of Sa'id ibn al-Musayyab (15–94 AH) is among the most detailed. Sa'id was the son-in-law of Abu Hurayra and one of the most authoritative legal scholars of Madinah. He was known for his independent judgment and his willingness to oppose the Umayyad caliphs when he judged their actions contrary to Islamic law — a stance that led to his imprisonment and flogging.

Ibn Sa'd's biography of Sa'id preserves traditions about his legal opinions, his personality, his famous statement that he considered nothing worse than a Muslim who does not know the laws of his religion, and the tradition that he never missed the opening takbir of a prayer in forty years. These biographical traditions combine legal and devotional significance in a way characteristic of the best early Islamic biographical writing.

Hasan al-Basri

The biography of Hasan al-Basri (21–110 AH) is one of the most important in Tabaqat al-Kabir, reflecting his enormous influence on early Islamic spirituality, theology, and legal thought. Hasan was born in Madinah, grew up in the household of Umm Salama (a wife of the Prophet), and eventually settled in Basra where he became the foremost scholar of his generation. Ibn Sa'd's biography preserves traditions about his preaching style, his theological positions on free will and divine justice (he was accused by some of Mutazilite leanings), and the spiritual counsels for which he was famous throughout the Islamic world.
content_id:
Biografi-biografi para Tabi'in — generasi Muslim yang belajar langsung dari para Sahabat tetapi tidak mengenal Nabi ﷺ secara pribadi — menempati bagian substansial dari Thabaqat al-Kabir. Generasi ini sangat penting untuk transmisi ilmu Islam, karena mereka adalah penerima dan transmiter utama ilmu para Sahabat dan guru-guru dari para pengumpul hadis besar abad ke-3 H.

Definisi Tabi'in

Ibn Sa'd sangat tepat tentang definisi Penerus (Tabi'in). Seseorang dihitung di antara tabi'in jika mereka bertemu setidaknya satu Sahabat sebagai Muslim (yaitu, dalam kapasitas Muslim, bukan sekadar sebagai pertemuan historis). Batasan untuk dihitung sebagai Sahabat juga tepat: seseorang harus telah bertemu Nabi ﷺ sebagai Muslim dan wafat sebagai Muslim. Pertanyaan-pertanyaan definitif ini memiliki konsekuensi praktis untuk evaluasi hadis, karena status Sahabat membawa prasangka keandalan penuh yang status Tabi'i tidak miliki.

Para Tabi'in bukanlah kelompok yang homogen. Mereka mencakup ulama-ulama besar Madinah (Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, Urwah ibn az-Zubair, Nafi' bekas budak Ibn Umar, dan lainnya) yang menjadi sumber tradisi hukum Madinah; ulama-ulama Makkah (Mujahid, Atha' ibn Abi Rabah, Thawus, Ikrimah) yang membentuk mazhab hukum Makkah; ulama-ulama Kufah (Ibrahim an-Nakha'i, Alqamah ibn Qais, asy-Sya'bi) yang menjadi sumber tradisi Irak/Hanafi; dan ulama-ulama Bashrah (Hasan al-Bashri, Muhammad ibn Sirin, rekan-rekan sezaman Ibn Sirin) yang membentuk salah satu pusat beasiswa Islam awal yang paling kaya.

Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib

Di antara biografi-biografi Penerus dalam Thabaqat al-Kabir, biografi Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib (15–94 H) adalah yang paling terperinci. Sa'id adalah menantu Abu Hurairah dan salah satu ulama hukum paling otoritatif di Madinah. Beliau dikenal karena penilaian independennya dan kesediaannya untuk menentang khalifah-khalifah Umayyah ketika ia menilai tindakan mereka bertentangan dengan hukum Islam — sikap yang menyebabkan penjaraannya dan dihukum cambuk.

Biografi Sa'id karya Ibn Sa'd melestarikan tradisi-tradisi tentang pendapat-pendapat hukumnya, kepribadiannya, pernyataannya yang terkenal bahwa ia menganggap tidak ada yang lebih buruk daripada seorang Muslim yang tidak mengetahui hukum-hukum agamanya, dan tradisi bahwa ia tidak pernah ketinggalan takbiratul ihram sebuah salat dalam empat puluh tahun.

Hasan al-Bashri

Biografi Hasan al-Bashri (21–110 H) adalah salah satu yang paling penting dalam Thabaqat al-Kabir, mencerminkan pengaruhnya yang sangat besar pada spiritualitas, teologi, dan pemikiran hukum Islam awal. Hasan lahir di Madinah, tumbuh dalam rumah tangga Ummu Salamah (istri Nabi ﷺ), dan akhirnya menetap di Bashrah di mana ia menjadi ulama terkemuka generasinya. Biografi Ibn Sa'd melestarikan tradisi-tradisi tentang gaya berceramahnya, posisi-posisi teologisnya tentang kehendak bebas dan keadilan ilahi, dan nasihat-nasihat spiritual yang membuatnya terkenal di seluruh dunia Islam.
